comments.text:
This is a neat exploration, but I think the framing glosses over a real tension that Julia and Zig have both had to grapple with in practice.

The dynamic-then-static approach sounds elegant in theory, but it only works well if your dynamic checks are truly cheap and your static compiler can eliminate them reliably. Julia's experience has been that neither always holds. You end up with code paths where the compiler can't prove the checks away, and then you're left running expensive type dispatch at runtime in your "fast" code. This is why Julia has drifted toward requiring more upfront type annotations despite its dynamic philosophy.

Zig took the opposite bet, saying "no, we just refuse to compile the dynamic case at all" which is simpler but painful in practice for things like REPL prototyping or dynamic metaprogramming.

The borrow-checking angle here is interesting though. In a dynamically-typed world, you can't do the static analysis that Rust does, so runtime borrow checks make sense. But the tradeoff is that your interior pointers, stack allocation, and single ownership have to be checked at runtime, which means they're really only "cheap" if the dynamic type information is already in the hot path (which it usually is in dynamic languages). You're not really gaining much over just using reference counting or GC at that point.

What would be the killer app

kids.text: I ran into exactly this with Julia around 2016 when we were trying to use it for a numerical library. The compiler would refuse to eliminate the dispatch checks in tight loops, and profiling showed we were spending 40% of runtime in type resolution. Switched to Rust for that component and the problem

Rust didn't eliminate the dispatch overhead, you just moved it to compile-time. Julia's real problem was that the compiler couldn't prove `@inbounds` safety in hot loops without type stability. Those are different problems. For a numerical library, a few type annotations upfront would have solved it without

comments.text:
This reminds me of how Lua approached the problem back in the 90s - you had a simple dynamic core with explicit type annotations bolted on top for performance. The difference here is that borrow-checking adds another layer of complexity on top of that already-tricky dual-mode system.

The nostalgic angle is worth mentioning: languages like Dylan and Cecil tried similar layering (dynamic base, optional static typing) and ran into the problem that most real code ends up needing both at once. You can't really isolate "this module is dynamic" and "this module is static" when they call each other.

That said, this approach feels closer to what Cython actually does well in practice - you write Python, you opt into C-like annotations where it matters, and the toolchain figures out the rest. The borrow-checker wrinkle here is novel though. Checking borrows at runtime with useful

kids.text: The irony is that we keep rediscovering this same tension every decade. Dylan failed, Cecil failed, and now we're watching Zig and Julia dance around it. At some point the dual-mode hypothesis itself might be the problem.

I think the dual-mode hypothesis works better than the history suggests, but only if you accept that "both at once" isn't a failure-it's the point. Julia's approach (statically verify what you can, dynamically check the rest) actually sidesteps the Dylan/Cecil problem because the language

Dylan's a good example here. The vision was elegant on paper, but you hit the wall pretty fast when your hot path needs static guarantees and your cold path needs reflection. The dual-mode thing only works if you can cleanly partition them, which in practice you can't.

What's interesting about this

I actually tried implementing something similar last year with a Lisp variant. The dual-mode partition breaks down fast once you have callbacks and closures crossing the boundary. Dylan's real problem wasn't the layering itself, but that you can't statically prove which mode a closure needs without whole-program analysis.

comments.text: This is exactly the kind of work that should inform language-design standards bodies. Right now we're stuck in a false binary between "fully static" and "fully dynamic," and the tooling communities around both camps have basically accepted that tradeoff as inevitable. Agreed on the terminology. The docs call it "dynamic borrow-checking" in a few places, but that's still confusing since the word "borrow" in the PL community specifically means static lifetime analysis. What you're doing here is more like runtime reference-tracking or ownership validation. Cle

kids.text:
I've been using Lua for years in game engines and you're spot on about metatables. The flexibility is genuinely useful when you're prototyping game behavior or handling data that doesn't fit a rigid schema. But I've hit the same wall repeatedly: you write this elegant dynamic code, it ships, then profiling shows 40% of CPU time is in type dispatch and metamethod lookups.

What's interesting about the Zest approach is that it sidesteps the "guess and recompile" problem. In Julia you end up with these mystery hotspots where the compiler chose the wrong specialization path and now you're stuck with dynamic checks in production. With explicit opt-in, you at least know where you're paying the cost.

I built a small trading system in Lua a few years back. The entry point was beautiful - dynamic, expressive, easy to reload during market hours. But the tick processor? That ran on every price update. Converting it to statically typed C was painful, but the 10x speedup was necessary. If Zest lets you

I actually tested this approach in a small interpreter last year. The dynamic borrow-checking overhead was negligible (< 2% in my benchmarks) because the checks are just pointer comparisons and a stack walk, not full type introspection. The real win is that you catch use-after-free

comments.text:
I actually built something similar to this last year for a research project. The key insight about runtime borrow-checking being "fairly cheap" is real, but I wanted to verify the claim with actual numbers.

I instrumented a small dynamically-typed interpreter with borrow tracking and ran it against a few tight

kids.text:
I built something in this space too - a Scheme interpreter with runtime borrow tracking for a systems course project. The performance overhead was actually surprising, though. We measured it at about 12-15% on synthetic workloads, which felt reasonable until we hit real code with lots of iterator patterns. Then it jumped to 30-40%.

The interesting thing we found was that the error messages were the real win, not the performance. Being able to point at the exact line where a borrow violation happened at runtime, with a full stack trace, made debugging so much faster than static errors that take 10 minutes to parse.

If you're interested in this approach, you might look at what Sorbet does for Ruby - it's not borrow-checking but the idea of layering static guarantees on top of a dynamic runtime is similar. Their approach to gradual typing might inform some of the design choices here.

https://sorbet.
